_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers cross-checked against NCCN AIDS-related KS guideline 2024, Krown 1997 ACTG staging, Northfelt 1998 liposomal doxorubicin trial, IAS-USA 2022 antiretroviral recommendations, and AIDS Malignancy Consortium consensus.

h1Title: Pulmonary Kaposi Sarcoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma is an HHV-8-driven angioproliferative tumor of the lung that arises almost exclusively in people with advanced immunosuppression, most often untreated HIV with a CD4 count below 200 cells/microliter. The lungs are involved in roughly 20-50% of patients with AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma and the pulmonary form is the most aggressive presentation, with a median survival of 2-10 months when left untreated. Patients present with dry cough, progressive dyspnea, fever, and sometimes massive hemoptysis or hemorrhagic pleural effusions. The disease has shrunk dramatically since combination antiretroviral therapy entered routine use in 1996, falling from a 50% lifetime incidence in pre-HAART AIDS to under 5% today, with substantially better survival when antiretroviral therapy and systemic chemotherapy are started together.

keyStats.prevalence: Pulmonary involvement in 20-50% of AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma cases; overall AIDS-KS incidence under 5% in the post-HAART era versus 50% pre-1996
keyStats.demographics: Men who have sex with men disproportionately affected; near-universal in patients with CD4 <100 cells/microliter and detectable HIV viral load
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases age 30-55 in AIDS-associated form; transplant-associated cases mirror transplant recipient age (40-65)
keyStats.globalCases: Sub-Saharan Africa carries the highest burden; KS is the most common cancer in men in several African countries with high HIV prevalence

definition: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma (ICD-10: C46.5) is a multifocal angioproliferative neoplasm of the lung parenchyma, airways, pleura, and mediastinal lymph nodes caused by Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus, also called human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8). The tumor arises from lymphatic endothelial cells transformed by HHV-8 latent gene products, particularly LANA-1 and viral cyclin, in the setting of immune dysregulation. Four epidemiologic forms exist: epidemic (AIDS-associated, the form that produces nearly all pulmonary cases), iatrogenic (in organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors), classic (older Mediterranean and Eastern European men), and endemic African (which can be aggressive in children). Pulmonary involvement is one of four AIDS-defining presentations of Kaposi sarcoma alongside cutaneous, oral, and visceral gastrointestinal disease and is the most lethal form when untreated. The American Joint Committee on Cancer does not stage AIDS-KS by TNM; instead the AIDS Clinical Trials Group (ACTG) classification developed by Krown et al. in 1997 stratifies patients by T (tumor extent), I (immune system, anchored on CD4 count), and S (systemic illness). Pulmonary KS is automatically classified as T1 (poor risk) regardless of other findings, reflecting its aggressive natural history. Care is delivered jointly by oncology, pulmonology, and infectious disease specialists familiar with HIV.

typesClassification.description: Nearly all pulmonary cases. Occurs in people living with HIV, almost always with CD4 <200 cells/microliter and detectable viral load. Often presents with concurrent cutaneous, oral, and visceral lesions.

Develops in solid-organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus). Onset typically within 2 years of transplant; reducing immunosuppression and switching to sirolimus can produce regression.
Rare. Occurs in older men of Mediterranean, Eastern European, or Ashkenazi Jewish descent. Lung involvement is uncommon and usually a late complication after years of cutaneous disease.
Aggressive form seen in HIV-negative adults and children in equatorial Africa. Pulmonary disease in the African pediatric variant carries a particularly poor prognosis.

primarySymptoms: Progressive shortness of breath developing over weeks to a few months, often disproportionate to physical exam findings and not responsive to bronchodilators or antibiotics.
A persistent dry, nonproductive cough that worsens with exertion and may become the dominant complaint before imaging is obtained.
Fever, drenching night sweats, and weight loss as part of the systemic illness pattern in patients with advanced HIV and high tumor burden.
Hemoptysis ranging from blood-streaked sputum to massive pulmonary hemorrhage from friable endobronchial KS plaques eroding bronchial vessels.
Pleural effusions on one or both sides that are often serosanguinous or frankly bloody, exudative, and KSHV-positive on cytology and PCR.
Substernal chest pain or dull pleuritic discomfort that worsens with deep breathing, more common when pleural disease or large mediastinal nodes are present.
Hoarseness, stridor, or wheezing when endobronchial lesions partially obstruct the trachea or a main bronchus.
Visible violet-red or brown-purple skin lesions on the legs, face, trunk, or hard palate accompany pulmonary disease in over 90% of cases and are often what prompts referral.
Lymphadenopathy in the neck, axilla, or groin reflecting disseminated KS or co-occurring multicentric Castleman disease.
Rapid clinical deterioration with respiratory failure over days when untreated pulmonary KS progresses unchecked, sometimes mistaken for opportunistic pneumonia.
earlyWarningSigns: A new dry cough or unexplained dyspnea in any person with HIV and CD4 <200 cells/microliter, particularly if cutaneous KS is already known
Skin or oral mucosal lesions that bleed easily, darken over weeks, or appear in a typical KS distribution (lower legs, hard palate, tip of the nose)
Unilateral or bilateral pleural effusion in a person with HIV that recurs after drainage and does not match an infectious profile
Sustained low oxygen saturation on room air in an HIV-positive patient when a chest X-ray shows perihilar reticulonodular opacities
emergencySigns: Massive hemoptysis (greater than 200 mL in 24 hours) — life-threatening bleeding from friable endobronchial KS requiring urgent bronchoscopy and possibly bronchial artery embolization

Acute hypoxic respiratory failure with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and SpO2 below 90% on room air in an HIV-positive patient — admission for oxygen, broad-spectrum coverage, and urgent oncology consult
Rapidly enlarging cervical or mediastinal lymphadenopathy with stridor or superior vena cava syndrome — airway compromise that may require corticosteroids and emergent radiotherapy
Sudden worsening of pulmonary KS within the first 8-12 weeks of starting antiretroviral therapy — possible immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (KS-IRIS) requiring chemotherapy escalation

causes.description: HHV-8 is necessary for every case of Kaposi sarcoma, including the pulmonary form. The virus latently infects lymphatic endothelial cells and drives tumorigenesis through viral genes that mimic cellular cyclin, FLIP, and chemokines. Without HHV-8 there is no Kaposi sarcoma.

Loss of CD4 T-cell control allows HHV-8 to escape latent suppression and drive endothelial proliferation. Risk rises sharply once CD4 falls below 200 cells/microliter and is highest below 100 cells/microliter.

Cyclosporine and tacrolimus suppress the same anti-HHV-8 immunity. Up to 5% of organ transplant recipients in endemic regions develop KS; reducing immunosuppression or switching to mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus) often produces regression.

causes.cause: Chronic immune dysregulation in classic and endemic forms
causes.description: Older Mediterranean adults and HIV-negative people in equatorial Africa develop KS through age-related or environment-related immune drift, with HHV-8 reactivation as the proximate trigger. Lung involvement in these forms is uncommon but described.

causes.cause: Co-infection with HIV in HHV-8 carriers
causes.description: The combination is multiplicative. HHV-8 seroprevalence is 5-20% in the US general population but 30-60% in men who have sex with men; pairing this with untreated HIV produces the majority of pulmonary KS cases worldwide.

riskFactors.factor: Untreated or virologically unsuppressed HIV infection
riskFactors.description: Detectable HIV viral load and CD4 below 200 cells/microliter are the dominant risk drivers. Effective antiretroviral therapy reduces AIDS-KS incidence by over 90%.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex and male-to-male sexual contact
riskFactors.description: HHV-8 transmits efficiently through saliva and sexual contact in MSM populations; epidemic KS incidence in this group remains 5-10 times higher than in heterosexual men with HIV.
Serologic evidence of HHV-8 infection is a prerequisite. Seroprevalence varies from under 5% in Northern Europe to over 50% in equatorial Africa and Mediterranean populations.

riskFactors.factor: Solid-organ transplant with calcineurin inhibitor immunosuppression
riskFactors.description: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus carry the highest KS risk; sirolimus and other mTOR inhibitors are associated with lower KS incidence and can induce regression.

riskFactors.factor: Older age in Mediterranean and Eastern European men
riskFactors.description: Classic KS occurs in men over 60 from these regions, reflecting both higher HHV-8 prevalence and age-related immune senescence.
Endemic HHV-8 transmission begins in childhood through saliva; high background prevalence combined with high HIV prevalence produces the world's highest KS burden.

riskFactors.factor: Low CD4 nadir during HIV care
riskFactors.description: A historical CD4 nadir under 100 cells/microliter raises future KS risk even after immune reconstitution, reflecting persistent HHV-8 reservoir activity.

affectedDemographics: AIDS-related KS incidence has fallen from ~50% of AIDS patients pre-1996 to under 5% in the post-HAART era in countries with good ART access
Sub-Saharan Africa accounts for the majority of global KS cases; KS is the most common cancer in adult men in Uganda, Zimbabwe, and Malawi
In high-income countries, MSM living with HIV remain the dominant epidemic-KS population; transplant-associated KS is rising as transplantation expands
Pulmonary involvement occurs in 20-50% of AIDS-KS cases with extensive cutaneous and visceral disease; isolated pulmonary KS without skin lesions is rare (<10%)
Pediatric endemic KS in equatorial Africa typically presents with lymphadenopathy and aggressive visceral disease rather than skin lesions
diagnosisOverview: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma is diagnosed through a combination of clinical context, imaging, bronchoscopy, and supportive virologic and immunologic testing. The decisive clinical clue is a person with known or newly diagnosed HIV, a low CD4 count, and cutaneous or oral KS who develops respiratory symptoms. Chest CT shows a highly characteristic pattern of flame-shaped peribronchovascular nodules, peribronchovascular interstitial thickening, parenchymal opacities radiating along bronchovascular bundles, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and pleural effusions. Bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and prognostic: it reveals raised cherry-red or violaceous endobronchial plaques that are essentially pathognomonic in an HIV-positive patient with cutaneous KS, and many clinicians accept this visual appearance as sufficient for diagnosis without biopsy because transbronchial biopsy of vascular lesions risks hemorrhage and is often non-diagnostic due to sampling error. Pleural effusions, if present, are sent for cytology, cell count, KSHV PCR, and culture to exclude opportunistic infection. Gallium-67 and thallium-201 nuclear scans help differentiate KS (gallium-negative, thallium-positive) from concurrent opportunistic pneumonia (gallium-positive). Supporting laboratory work always includes CD4 count, HIV viral load, KSHV serology, and plasma KSHV viral load, which correlates with tumor burden. HIV-negative patients warrant a transplant-history review or, in older Mediterranean men, classic-KS workup. Pulmonary KS is always classified as ACTG T1 (poor risk).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic peribronchovascular flame-shaped nodules, parenchymal opacities, lymphadenopathy, and pleural effusions. Distinguishes pulmonary KS from PJP, MAC, lymphoma, and bacterial pneumonia by distribution pattern.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-contrast or contrast-enhanced CT lasting 5-10 minutes. The radiologist looks specifically for nodules following bronchovascular bundles rather than the random or perilymphatic pattern of opportunistic infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualizes the raised cherry-red or violaceous endobronchial plaques that are essentially diagnostic of pulmonary KS in an HIV-positive patient with cutaneous disease. Also allows bronchoalveolar lavage to exclude PJP, CMV, and bacterial pneumonia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A pulmonologist passes a thin scope through the nose or mouth into the airways under conscious sedation. The procedure takes 20-30 minutes. Biopsy of vascular plaques is often avoided because of bleeding risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed when the diagnosis is unclear, when no cutaneous lesions are present, or when alternative diagnoses cannot be excluded. Histology shows spindle cells, slit-like vascular spaces, and HHV-8 LANA-1 immunostaining.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during bronchoscopy. Small forceps biopsies are taken; sampling yield is roughly 30-50% because of patchy disease and bleeding risk from highly vascular lesions.

diagnosticTests.test: Pleural fluid analysis with KSHV PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Pleural effusions in pulmonary KS are typically exudative, serosanguinous, and KSHV-positive by PCR. Cytology may show atypical spindle cells. Rules out primary effusion lymphoma, parapneumonic effusion, and tuberculosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Thoracentesis under ultrasound guidance, removing 30-1000 mL of fluid for cell count, chemistry, cytology, Gram stain, culture, AFB, and KSHV PCR. Procedure time 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: CD4 count and HIV viral load
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes immune status, guides ART selection, and contributes to ACTG staging. Pulmonary KS almost always occurs with CD4 below 200 and detectable HIV RNA in untreated patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw. Results typically return within 1-3 days. Repeat testing is used to monitor immune reconstitution on ART.

diagnosticTests.test: KSHV (HHV-8) serology and plasma viral load
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms HHV-8 infection (a prerequisite) and quantifies viral activity. Plasma KSHV viral load correlates with tumor burden and tracks response to chemotherapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw sent to a specialty virology laboratory. Results return within 1-2 weeks. Repeat testing may be used to monitor treatment response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates KS (thallium-positive, gallium-negative) from opportunistic pulmonary infection (gallium-positive) when biopsy is high-risk or non-diagnostic. Less commonly used now where PET-CT or DECT is available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Injection of a radiotracer followed by whole-body scanning 4-72 hours later. Each scan takes 30-60 minutes. Radiation dose is comparable to a standard CT.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma rests on two pillars used together: combination antiretroviral therapy to restore immunity and suppress HHV-8 reactivation, and systemic chemotherapy to reduce the lethal tumor burden in the lungs. NCCN guidelines for AIDS-related KS, the AIDS Malignancy Consortium consensus, and IAS-USA recommendations all classify pulmonary involvement as an indication for immediate systemic chemotherapy rather than ART alone. First-line chemotherapy is pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil) at 20 mg/m squared intravenously every three weeks; this regimen produced response rates of 55-60% in the Northfelt 1998 pivotal trial and remains the global standard. Paclitaxel at 100 mg/m squared every two weeks is the principal alternative and has equivalent efficacy in head-to-head data, with the trade-off of more neuropathy and alopecia. ART must be initiated or optimized in parallel; integrase-inhibitor-based regimens (bictegravir, dolutegravir) achieve rapid viral suppression and faster immune reconstitution. Clinicians remain alert for KS immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (KS-IRIS), which paradoxically worsens KS in the first 8-12 weeks of ART in roughly 10-30% of patients and can require chemotherapy escalation, corticosteroids, or in severe cases brief ART interruption. For relapsed or refractory disease, pomalidomide gained FDA approval in 2020 based on response rates near 70% in chemotherapy-refractory patients. Local measures play a supporting role: radiotherapy controls bulky symptomatic lesions and mediastinal disease, bronchial artery embolization treats massive hemoptysis, intralesional vinblastine and topical alitretinoin manage skin lesions, and palliative care addresses dyspnea and pain. Transplant-associated cases respond to reduction of immunosuppression and conversion to sirolimus in addition to chemotherapy when needed.

medicalTreatments.name: Combination antiretroviral therapy (e.g., bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide, dolutegravir-based regimens)
medicalTreatments.description: Foundational and lifelong. Suppresses HIV replication, restores CD4 T-cell counts, and reactivates anti-HHV-8 immune control. Started or optimized as soon as pulmonary KS is diagnosed, in parallel with chemotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces AIDS-KS incidence by over 90% at the population level; in established pulmonary KS, ART alone produces regression in mild cases but is insufficient as monotherapy for pulmonary involvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil 20 mg/m squared IV every 3 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic chemotherapy for pulmonary, visceral, or rapidly progressive KS. Liposomal encapsulation concentrates drug in tumor tissue and reduces cardiac toxicity. Typically given for 6-8 cycles, with reassessment by imaging and clinical response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rate 55-60% in the Northfelt 1998 trial; symptom improvement in 80%+ of pulmonary KS patients when paired with effective ART.

medicalTreatments.name: Paclitaxel (100 mg/m squared IV every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Equivalent first-line alternative to liposomal doxorubicin per NCCN. Preferred when doxorubicin is contraindicated (cardiac dysfunction, prior anthracycline exposure). Higher rates of neuropathy, alopecia, and myelosuppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 56-71% in AIDS-KS trials; comparable to liposomal doxorubicin in randomized head-to-head data (AMC 062 trial).

medicalTreatments.name: Pomalidomide (5 mg orally daily for 21 of 28 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Immunomodulatory agent approved by the FDA in 2020 for AIDS-related and HIV-negative Kaposi sarcoma after at least one prior systemic therapy. Mechanism involves direct antitumor effect plus restoration of HHV-8-specific T-cell responses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 71% (HIV-positive) and 67% (HIV-negative) in the NCI pivotal study; durable responses lasting 12 months or more in many patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Radiotherapy (8-30 Gy in 1-15 fractions to symptomatic field)
medicalTreatments.description: Local palliation for bulky mediastinal disease, obstructive endobronchial lesions, symptomatic cutaneous KS, or oral and pharyngeal lesions causing dysphagia. Single-fraction 8 Gy is often sufficient for cutaneous palliation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 80-90% of treated sites; not curative for pulmonary disease but useful for emergency airway compression or hemoptysis control.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional vinblastine for cutaneous lesions
medicalTreatments.description: Direct injection of vinblastine (0.1-0.2 mg/mL) into individual cutaneous or oral KS lesions. Useful for cosmetically important areas (face, hands) when systemic chemotherapy is not yet indicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Local response rate 70-90% per lesion; recurrence is common without concurrent systemic therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Sirolimus conversion in transplant-associated KS
medicalTreatments.description: For organ transplant recipients with iatrogenic KS, switching from a calcineurin inhibitor to sirolimus combines maintenance of graft function with direct antitumor activity through mTOR inhibition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial cutaneous regression in 60-75% of renal transplant patients within 6 months of conversion; less robust data in pulmonary cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids for severe KS-IRIS
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course prednisone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day with taper) is used cautiously for severe immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome despite a paradoxical risk of worsening KS, alongside chemotherapy escalation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in moderate-to-severe IRIS; expert-only use because steroids alone can flare KS.

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchial artery embolization for massive hemoptysis
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiology procedure to occlude bleeding bronchial arteries supplying friable endobronchial KS. Performed under fluoroscopy with selective catheterization and embolic agents (gelfoam, microspheres, coils).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate hemoptysis control in 85-90% of cases; rebleeding within 6 months in 20-30% unless underlying disease is controlled.

surgicalOptions.name: Therapeutic thoracentesis or indwelling pleural catheter
surgicalOptions.description: Drainage of large symptomatic pleural effusions for dyspnea relief. Recurrent effusions may benefit from a tunneled indwelling catheter or talc pleurodesis when systemic therapy has not yet controlled disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 80-95% of drained patients; pleurodesis success around 60-70% in malignant effusions.

surgicalOptions.name: Endobronchial laser or argon plasma coagulation
surgicalOptions.description: Bronchoscopic ablation of obstructing endobronchial KS lesions to restore airway patency. Used for central airway tumors causing stridor or post-obstructive atelectasis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate airway opening in over 80% of selected cases; durable benefit depends on systemic control.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical alitretinoin 0.1% gel for cutaneous lesions
alternativeTreatments.description: Approved retinoid gel applied twice daily to small cutaneous KS lesions. Useful adjunct for cosmetic lesions and partial palliation. No role in pulmonary disease but commonly used in patients with both.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation and pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Tobacco smoke worsens dyspnea and accelerates loss of lung function in patients with pulmonary KS. Structured pulmonary rehab improves exercise tolerance, dyspnea scores, and quality of life during and after chemotherapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma requires coordinated specialist care from the start: an HIV-experienced infectious disease physician to manage ART and KS-IRIS, a medical oncologist familiar with AIDS-related malignancies to direct liposomal doxorubicin or paclitaxel, and a pulmonologist for bronchoscopic diagnosis and airway control. Transplant-associated cases need close work with the transplant team to safely adjust immunosuppression. Centers with an AIDS Malignancy Consortium or NCI-designated cancer center affiliation see the most cases and run the relevant clinical trials, particularly for refractory disease eligible for pomalidomide or immunotherapy protocols.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a medical oncologist with documented experience managing AIDS-related malignancies, ideally affiliated with a high-volume HIV program or a cancer center participating in the AIDS Malignancy Consortium. Ask whether the practice has integrated infectious disease support, access to pegylated liposomal doxorubicin and paclitaxel on formulary, bronchoscopy availability through pulmonology, and a pathway to interventional radiology for hemoptysis. For transplant patients, the oncologist should communicate directly with the transplant team about immunosuppression conversion to sirolimus.
preventionStrategies: Test for HIV at least annually if at higher risk (MSM, multiple partners, IV drug use, partners with HIV) and start antiretroviral therapy immediately upon diagnosis — this is the single most effective KS prevention strategy
Maintain ART adherence to keep HIV viral load undetectable and CD4 count above 200 cells/microliter, which prevents most cases of pulmonary KS
Use HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) in higher-risk individuals to prevent HIV acquisition and downstream KS risk
In solid-organ transplant recipients with known HHV-8 seropositivity, consider sirolimus-based immunosuppression rather than calcineurin inhibitors where graft-specific evidence supports it
Screen for cutaneous and oral KS at every HIV clinic visit so that early lesions are caught before pulmonary spread; pulmonary KS rarely arises without prior or concurrent cutaneous disease
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking immediately; tobacco use worsens dyspnea and reduces tolerance of chemotherapy in pulmonary KS
Maintain adherence to ART, KS chemotherapy, and prophylactic antimicrobials (PJP, MAC, toxoplasmosis prophylaxis as indicated by CD4 count)
Address nutrition — patients with AIDS-KS often have weight loss and sarcopenia that worsens chemotherapy tolerance; structured dietitian input improves outcomes
Avoid live vaccines while immunosuppressed; ensure pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19, and (where indicated) HPV vaccinations are up to date
Mental health support is essential — depression, anxiety, and HIV stigma are common and reduce treatment adherence
Engage social work early for medication access, insurance navigation, and Ryan White program enrollment in the US where applicable

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to counter cachexia and support immune reconstitution
Energy-dense foods if weight loss is significant — full-fat dairy, nut butters, avocado, olive oil
Cooked vegetables, well-washed fruit, and pasteurized dairy to reduce foodborne infection risk during chemotherapy
Adequate hydration, especially during liposomal doxorubicin or paclitaxel cycles, to reduce mucositis and nausea
Oral nutritional supplements between meals during cycles when appetite is poor
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat, eggs, and seafood while immunosuppressed — risk of Listeria, Salmonella, and Vibrio infections
Unpasteurized dairy and soft cheeses made from raw milk
Alcohol while on doxorubicin or paclitaxel — hepatic toxicity is potentiated
Grapefruit and Seville orange products if on calcineurin inhibitors or selected antiretrovirals — they alter drug levels significantly
Crash dieting or extreme weight loss during chemotherapy — sarcopenia worsens chemotherapy tolerance

exerciseGuidelines: Tolerance varies by tumor burden and chemotherapy phase. Light-to-moderate aerobic activity — walking, stationary cycling, gentle yoga — for 20-30 minutes most days is encouraged when oxygen saturation is stable and there is no active hemoptysis. Resistance work twice weekly preserves muscle mass during chemotherapy. Pulmonary rehabilitation is a structured option for patients with persistent dyspnea after treatment. Avoid strenuous exertion during cycles of nadir blood counts.
dailyManagement: Take antiretroviral therapy at the same time daily; missed doses risk viral rebound and KS progression
Monitor for shortness of breath, new fevers, blood in sputum, and weight loss and report changes within 24 hours during chemotherapy cycles
Keep a current medication list including ART, chemotherapy schedule, and prophylactic agents — drug-drug interactions with antiretrovirals are common
Attend every infusion appointment; delays in chemotherapy worsen outcomes more than mild side effects
Use sun protection on cutaneous KS lesions; UV exposure can darken and worsen the appearance of lesions
Schedule dental review before starting chemotherapy if possible to reduce mucositis and infection risk
prognosis: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma was a near-uniformly fatal illness before combination antiretroviral therapy, with median survival under 6 months and pre-HAART 6-month survival below 30%. With contemporary ART plus liposomal doxorubicin or paclitaxel, 1-year survival reaches roughly 70% and 5-year survival approaches 50% in well-resourced settings, though outcomes remain markedly worse in regions without consistent ART access. Prognosis is strongest when ART produces durable virologic suppression and CD4 recovery, when systemic chemotherapy achieves an objective response within the first 2-3 cycles, and when the patient has no opportunistic infections at presentation. The AIDS Clinical Trials Group (ACTG) staging system — T (tumor extent, with pulmonary KS automatically T1), I (immune system, CD4 cutoff of 150 cells/microliter), and S (systemic illness) — remains the most widely used prognostic framework. Pomalidomide and emerging immunotherapy options are improving outcomes in relapsed disease.
recoveryTimeline: Symptomatic improvement on chemotherapy plus ART typically begins within 4-8 weeks. Most patients see objective radiographic response by cycle 3-4 of liposomal doxorubicin (roughly week 9-12). Complete radiographic remission is uncommon; durable partial response with stable scarring is the typical end point. ART-driven immune reconstitution continues over 6-24 months. KS-IRIS, if it occurs, peaks in the first 8-12 weeks of ART and usually settles by month 6 with chemotherapy escalation. Long-term follow-up imaging and KSHV viral load monitoring continue for years given the relapse risk if HIV suppression is lost.
complications: Massive hemoptysis from friable endobronchial KS — life-threatening and a common cause of death in untreated pulmonary disease
Acute hypoxic respiratory failure requiring intensive care, particularly in patients presenting late with bilateral pulmonary involvement
Kaposi sarcoma immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (KS-IRIS) in the first 8-12 weeks of ART, occurring in 10-30% of patients
Recurrent malignant pleural effusions requiring repeated drainage or indwelling catheters
Concurrent opportunistic infections (PJP, MAC, CMV, tuberculosis) that complicate diagnosis and chemotherapy delivery
Chemotherapy-related cardiotoxicity from cumulative doxorubicin exposure, neuropathy from paclitaxel, and myelosuppression
Primary effusion lymphoma and multicentric Castleman disease — other HHV-8-driven malignancies that can co-occur with KS and worsen prognosis

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — Kaposi Sarcoma Treatment (PDQ)
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient and clinician information on AIDS-related and other forms of Kaposi sarcoma, updated regularly by NCI.

supportResources.name: AIDS Malignancy Consortium
supportResources.description: NCI-funded clinical trials network for HIV-associated cancers including pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma; lists open trials and participating centers.

supportResources.name: HIV.gov — Living with HIV
supportResources.description: US Department of Health and Human Services resource on ART adherence, AIDS-defining illnesses, and support services.

confusedWithConditions.name: Opportunistic Pneumonia (PJP, bacterial, MAC)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia in advanced HIV usually presents with productive cough, fever, and a non-peribronchovascular CT pattern (ground-glass for PJP, cavitary for MAC, lobar for bacterial). KS produces peribronchovascular flame-shaped nodules and is thallium-positive, gallium-negative; opportunistic pneumonia is gallium-positive.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB classically causes upper-lobe cavitary disease, productive cough, and positive sputum AFB or NAAT; pulmonary KS is peribronchovascular, often basilar, and AFB-negative. Both can coexist in advanced HIV, so KS diagnosis does not exclude TB co-infection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Lymphoma (including primary effusion lymphoma)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma produces mass lesions, larger lymphadenopathy, and frequently a positive PET scan with high FDG uptake; KS has a more vascular, peribronchovascular pattern and is typically low or absent on FDG-PET. Both can be HHV-8 driven; primary effusion lymphoma is distinguished by malignant cells on pleural fluid cytology.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS without Kaposi sarcoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Many AIDS-defining illnesses cause pulmonary symptoms but lack the cutaneous and oral violet-red lesions, peribronchovascular CT pattern, and cherry-red endobronchial plaques that define pulmonary KS.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Virtually all pulmonary KS arises in advanced HIV; effective antiretroviral therapy is the central treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Opportunistic Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Common differential and co-occurrence in advanced HIV; must be excluded by bronchoalveolar lavage.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the immunosuppressed host; TB and pulmonary KS can coexist and complicate diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: HHV-8 also drives primary effusion lymphoma and multicentric Castleman disease, which can coexist with KS.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Alternative pulmonary neoplasm to consider in older patients; distinguished by histology, imaging pattern, and absence of cutaneous KS.

faqs.question: What is pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma is a cancer of lung blood and lymphatic vessels caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8). It occurs almost exclusively in people with severe immune suppression, most often advanced HIV, and is the most aggressive form of Kaposi sarcoma.

faqs.question: What causes Kaposi sarcoma in the lungs?
faqs.answer: It is caused by Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (HHV-8) in a person with weakened immunity, usually from untreated HIV or organ transplant immunosuppression. HHV-8 infects lymphatic endothelial cells and drives them to multiply when CD4 T-cell control fails.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk of pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Highest risk is in people with untreated HIV and a CD4 count below 200 cells per microliter, especially men who have sex with men. Solid-organ transplant recipients on cyclosporine or tacrolimus and older Mediterranean or sub-Saharan African men also carry elevated risk.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of Kaposi sarcoma in the lung?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include progressive shortness of breath, a persistent dry cough, low-grade fever, and sometimes blood-streaked sputum. Most patients also have skin or oral Kaposi sarcoma lesions before lung symptoms appear, usually with HIV and a low CD4 count.

faqs.question: How is pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical context, chest CT showing peribronchovascular flame-shaped nodules, and bronchoscopy showing raised cherry-red endobronchial plaques. In an HIV-positive patient with cutaneous KS, bronchoscopic appearance is often diagnostic without biopsy.

faqs.question: Is pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma curable?
faqs.answer: It is rarely cured but is often controllable for years with combination antiretroviral therapy and systemic chemotherapy. One-year survival with current treatment is around 70% and five-year survival approaches 50%. Lifelong HIV suppression is essential to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: What chemotherapy is used for pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: First-line chemotherapy is pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil) at 20 mg per square meter every three weeks, with response rates of 55-60%. Paclitaxel at 100 mg per square meter every two weeks is an equally effective alternative. Pomalidomide is approved for refractory disease.

faqs.question: Can antiretroviral therapy alone treat pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: ART is essential but not sufficient for pulmonary involvement. Mild cutaneous KS can regress on ART alone, but pulmonary disease is too aggressive and is treated with ART plus systemic chemotherapy from the start, as recommended by NCCN and the AIDS Malignancy Consortium.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, median survival is 2-10 months and 6-month survival is below 30%. With modern antiretroviral therapy plus chemotherapy, 1-year survival reaches about 70% and 5-year survival approaches 50% in well-resourced settings.

faqs.question: What is KS-IRIS and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: KS-IRIS is a paradoxical worsening of Kaposi sarcoma in the first 8-12 weeks after starting antiretroviral therapy. It occurs in 10-30% of patients with extensive KS and can require chemotherapy escalation. It does not mean ART has failed.

faqs.question: Does HHV-8 always cause Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: No. HHV-8 infection is necessary but not sufficient. Many people are infected with HHV-8 (5-50% depending on region) without ever developing Kaposi sarcoma. The disease appears when immune control of the virus is lost, most often through HIV or transplant immunosuppression.

faqs.question: Can Kaposi sarcoma develop in HIV-negative people?
faqs.answer: Yes. Classic Kaposi sarcoma occurs in older men of Mediterranean, Eastern European, or Ashkenazi Jewish descent. Endemic African Kaposi sarcoma occurs in HIV-negative adults and children in equatorial Africa. Iatrogenic Kaposi sarcoma occurs in transplant recipients.

faqs.question: How does pulmonary KS differ from PJP pneumonia?
faqs.answer: PJP causes diffuse ground-glass opacities on CT and productive cough, detected on bronchoalveolar lavage. Pulmonary KS produces peribronchovascular flame-shaped nodules, raised cherry-red endobronchial plaques, and is thallium-positive, gallium-negative on nuclear scan.

faqs.question: Is bronchoscopy biopsy required to diagnose pulmonary KS?
faqs.answer: Not always. In an HIV-positive patient with cutaneous KS, characteristic CT findings, and cherry-red endobronchial plaques on bronchoscopy, diagnosis is often accepted without biopsy because vascular lesions can bleed and biopsy yield is only 30-50%.

faqs.question: What does the AIDS Clinical Trials Group (ACTG) staging mean?
faqs.answer: The ACTG system stages AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma by tumor extent (T), immune status based on CD4 count (I), and systemic illness (S). Pulmonary involvement is automatically T1 (poor risk) because of its aggressive course, guiding chemotherapy decisions.

faqs.question: Can pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapse is common if HIV is not durably suppressed or if immunosuppression cannot be reduced in transplant patients. Ongoing ART, periodic CT imaging, and HHV-8 viral load monitoring help detect relapse early. Pomalidomide is available for recurrent disease.

faqs.question: How is massive hemoptysis from pulmonary KS treated?
faqs.answer: Massive hemoptysis is a medical emergency. Treatment includes airway protection, bronchoscopy to localize the bleeding source, and bronchial artery embolization to occlude bleeding vessels. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy follow once the patient is stabilized.

faqs.question: Does Kaposi sarcoma in transplant patients respond to changing medications?
faqs.answer: Often yes. Switching from a calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) to sirolimus produces complete or partial regression in 60-75% of renal transplant patients with cutaneous disease. Pulmonary involvement usually also needs systemic chemotherapy.

faqs.question: How is pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma different from lung cancer?
faqs.answer: Lung cancer arises from epithelial airway cells and typically presents as a discrete FDG-avid mass on PET. Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma arises from lymphatic endothelial cells, produces peribronchovascular flame-shaped nodules, and almost always occurs in advanced HIV.

faqs.question: Can pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma be prevented?
faqs.answer: The most effective prevention is early HIV diagnosis and immediate antiretroviral therapy to maintain viral suppression and CD4 count above 200. HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis prevents HIV acquisition. In transplant patients, sirolimus-based immunosuppression carries lower KS risk.

simpleName: Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8 cancer of the lungs)
